title: First-principles real-space embedding theory of the superconducting proximity effect
authors: Nicolas Baù, Mitra Dowlatabadi, Tommaso Chiarotti, Massimo Capone, Antimo Marrazzo
abstract: When a superconductor is placed in contact with a normal material, Cooper pairs penetrate the latter and induce superconductivity via the proximity effect. Despite its central role in quantum materials, superconducting devices and topological platforms, a predictive first-principles description of the proximity effect at realistic interfaces has remained computationally prohibitive so far. Here, we fill this gap by developing a Green's-function framework based on real-space dynamical embedding that enables first-principles simulations of superconducting proximity in mesoscopic systems. We show that the proximity effect admits a transparent diagrammatic formulation in terms of normal and anomalous embedding self-energies, which disentangle and quantify the distinct renormalization mechanisms generated by coupling to a superconducting bath. By combining this formalism with recursive schemes, we compute local spectral functions and proximity lengths extending over hundreds of nanometers into the bulk without resorting to thick interface slabs. We deploy the approach on tight-binding models (Qi-Hughes-Zhang and Fu-Kane-Mele), where we analyze mixed-parity superconductivity in topological insulators proximitized by $s$-wave superconductors, and on first-principles simulations of NbSe$_2$/CrBr$_3$ heterostructures based on density-functional theory and maximally-localized Wannier functions, the latter enabling direct comparison with scanning tunneling spectroscopy experiments. Our work provides a scalable and conceptually unified framework that bridges microscopic electronic structure and mesoscale proximity physics, enabling predictive atomistic simulations of superconducting interfaces.
summary: The authors present a new real-space embedding theory that allows for efficient, first-principles simulations of the superconducting proximity effect in large-scale mesoscopic systems. By decoupling microscopic electronic structure from mesoscopic physics, the method can capture proximity effects over hundreds of nanometers without the need for massive supercells. This provides a powerful tool for predicting the properties of topological and superconducting heterostructures.
main_problem: The difficulty of providing a predictive, first-principles description of the superconducting proximity effect at realistic interfaces due to the computational cost of simulating large, thick interface slabs.
main_result: The development of a scalable real-space dynamical embedding framework that bridges microscopic electronic structure and mesoscopic proximity physics, allowing for simulations of proximity effects extending hundreds of nanometers.
method: A Green's-function framework based on real-space dynamical embedding using normal and anomalous self-energies, integrated with DFT, Wannier functions, and recursive schemes.
model_or_system: The framework is applied to various systems including tight-binding models (QHZ and Fu-Kane-Mele), metal/superconductor interfaces, and first-principles simulations of NbSe2/CrBr3 van der Waals heterostructures.
key_observables: Local spectral functions, proximity lengths of singlet and triplet anomalous densities, Chern numbers, and layer-resolved density of states.
important_parameters: Interface coupling strength (Gamma), superconducting gap (Delta), spin-orbit coupling (SOC), and mesoscopic length scales.
main_assumptions: The theory assumes the ability to partition the system into subsystems and uses the Bogoliubov-de Gennes framework to describe the superconducting state.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of N/SC heterostructures; Figure 2 illustrates the diagrammatic representation of the two-step renormalization process; Figure 3 compares topological phase diagrams using different approaches; Figure 7 and 8 show the NbSe2/CrBr3 interface and comparison with experimental STS data.
paper_structure: The paper moves from the theoretical derivation of the embedding formalism to validation via tight-binding models (QHZ and FKM), followed by application to mesoscopic 3D systems, and concludes with first-principles simulations of real NbSe2/CrBr3 heterostructures.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of a normal–superconductor (N/SC) heterostructure used in the embedding scheme. Depending on the strength of the interface coupling, two limits can be identified. If the interface coupling is weak (left panel), the two constituents of the heterostructure can be studied separately. In this case, the isolated N material (region A) is described by a Green’s function G(A) 0 , while the isolated SC (region B) is described by its normal and anomalous propagators, G(B) 0 and F (B) 0 . Here, the SC (region B) plays the role of a bath and is formally integrated out, thereby effectively renormalizing the Green’s functions of the N subsystem (region A). The two regions are coupled by...

FIG. 2. Left panel (a): Diagrammatic representation of the embedding Dyson equations, Eqs. 6–10, that describe how the normal Green’s function G(A)(ω) and the induced anomalous Green’s function F (A)†(ω) of the normal subsystem A are renormalized by proximity to the superconductor B. First, the normal propagator of A is renormalized by the normal part of B, yielding the normally-embedded propagator G(A) ne (ω) through the normal embedding potential νemb no (ω); νemb no scales as h2 Γ (where hΓ is the single-particle interface coupling). Second, the fully renormalized G(A)(ω) is obtained from a Dyson equation for G(A) ne (ω) with a self-energy that contains products of the anomalous...

FIG. 3. Topological phase diagrams of a quantum anomalous Hall insulator (QAHI) in proximity of an s-wave superconductor (SC), as a function of the QAHI mass parameter and of the induced pairing. In the fixed-∆approach (left panel), a Bogoliubov- de Gennes Hamiltonian is constructed by considering the QHZ model and adding a constant pairing ∆[25]. In the right-hand panel, we report the exact solution of the embedding equations for a QAHI/SC heterostructure, where ∆sc is the bulk paring of the SC and Γ = 0.8 is the interface coupling. The center panel reports the solution of the embedding equations where the normal embedding potential is neglected (“anomalous only”). While three phases with...

FIG. 4. Topological phase diagrams obtained by solving the embedding equations for the QAHI/SC interface, model as in Fig. 3. In the top row (a), the value of the interface hopping Γ is fixed at three different values (left, center and right panels) while the phase diagram is studied with respect to the bulk pairing ∆sc. Vice versa, in the second row (b), ∆sc is fixed and the phase diagram is shown as function of Γ. The chiral topological phase with N = 1 becomes more stable for large values of the interlayer hopping. Notably, the m−Γ phase diagram qualitatively resembles that obtained with the fixed-∆approach (Fig. 4), where the dependence on ∆is hence better understood as a dependence on...

FIG. 5. Layer-resolved singlet anomalous density induced in a semi-infinite, single-band, free-electron-like metal (cubic lattice) by contact with a semi-infinite s-wave superconductor. The induced anomalous density is plotted as a function of the distance from the interface r⊥(measured in units of the lattice constant a). All data are normalized to the bulk s- wave anomalous density of the isolated superconductor, ψs sc. The two subsystems are coupled via nearest-neighbor hopping of amplitude Γ = 0.1 t, and the superconducting gap is ∆= 0.01 t.
